comments:
This manuscript describes the TwiSE team entry to SemEval-2016 Task 4 (Twitter sentiment analysis). The authors present a pragmatic system built from a collection of well-known components: careful preprocessing and tokenization, lexical and sentiment-lexicon features, hashed n-gram and character-gram representations, an α-power feature transformation, distributed word embeddings (when useful), and linear classifiers (Logistic Regression and linear SVM). For Subtask A they use stacked generalization over several tuned linear base models; for other subtasks they submit single tuned linear models (SVM or Logistic Regression). The paper reports respectable official rankings (9/35, 8/19, 1/11, 2/14 for A,B,C,D) and makes code available.

Strengths:
- The system is practical and reproducible: many implementation details are given and code is released. The feature set is comprehensive and follows successful prior work; the use of hashing and α-power transformation is well motivated and tuned per subtask.
- The stacking approach for Subtask A is sensible, and the authors explicitly use probabilistic outputs of base learners as stacking inputs, which is an empirically justified choice.
- Validation protocol is clearly described (training/validation/devtest steps) and class-weighting to address imbalance is used consistently.
- Reported official results demonstrate competitive performance, notably top ranking on Subtask C and strong placement on D.

Weaknesses and areas for improvement:
- Novelty is limited: the work assembles established features and standard classifiers rather than proposing new modelling ideas. The main contribution is an engineering/system report rather than methodological advance.
- The experimental analysis is somewhat shallow. There is no ablation study that quantifies the contribution of major components (lexicons vs. n-grams vs. embeddings vs. α-power transform vs. hashing). It is therefore hard to judge which parts of the pipeline drive performance.
- The authors explicitly ignore target/topic information for subtasks B/C/D and classify overall tweet polarity. This is an important methodological simplification for topic-specific subtasks and should be discussed more explicitly in terms of its impact and limitations.
- Although ordinal methods were evaluated, the decision to use multinomial Logistic Regression for Subtask C is only briefly justified; more detail on comparative performance and error types would strengthen the paper.
- The paper lacks thorough error analysis (e.g., where models fail: sarcasm, multiple targets, negation handling) and statistical significance testing to back claims of improvements.
- Some minor writing/formatting issues and repetitions appear in the provided text; a careful proofreading would improve readability.

Suggestions:
- Add an ablation study showing per-feature or per-module contribution to the main subtasks.
- Provide more detailed comparison between ordinal-specific approaches and the chosen multinomial approach (numbers, confusion patterns), and explain why embeddings were excluded in some subtasks.
- Include error analysis highlighting common failure modes and concrete directions to address them (e.g., target detection, improved negation scope, sarcasm handling).
- If available, report significance tests on dev/devtest to indicate which improvements are robust.

Overall assessment: this is a solid system description that will be useful to practitioners and participants in sentiment-analysis shared tasks. It is not methodologically novel but is competently executed and well validated on the SemEval dataset. The code release is an important plus for reproducibility.
